title: The Oscars Have Become Less Capable of Surprise
text: The article argues that Oscar predictions have become increasingly formulaic and predictable. According to the piece, current frontrunners for the 2026 Academy Awards ceremony largely mirror predictions made months earlier, before many contenders had even been released. The writer observes that "someone who made their Oscar winner predictions in July would probably be just as accurate as someone who updated their predictions after the nominees luncheon." The article highlights how industry insiders had already identified likely winners before films premiered: Paul Thomas Anderson and "One Battle After Another" were presumed Best Picture favorites from early on, Timothée Chalamet's Best Actor victory seemed predetermined, Jessie Buckley's "Hamnet" was widely expected to secure Best Actress. The only reliably unpredictable acting category is Best Supporting Actress, partly because performances by actors of color rarely carry advance "inevitability" buzz comparable to white performers.
